text.title: sshd Server Hardening Settings
text.summary: Critical sshd settings for Ubuntu/Debian VPS: key-only auth, access control, modern crypto — applied via drop-in file in sshd_config.d/ to survive package upgrades.

rule.statement: Always write custom SSH settings to /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf rather than editing /etc/ssh/sshd_config directly. Drop-in files survive OpenSSH package upgrades.
rule.rationale: Editing sshd_config directly risks losing changes on package upgrade. Drop-in files in sshd_config.d/ are merged alphabetically (later files win) and survive upgrades cleanly.

rule.statement: Set PasswordAuthentication no, ChallengeResponseAuthentication no, KbdInteractiveAuthentication no, and PubkeyAuthentication yes. Keep UsePAM yes for session management.
rule.rationale: Key-only auth eliminates brute-force password attacks entirely. ChallengeResponseAuthentication and KbdInteractiveAuthentication are alternative names for the same mechanism — both must be disabled to fully block keyboard-interactive login.

list.item: PermitRootLogin no — never allow direct root SSH
AllowUsers nero — whitelist specific users (space-separated for multiple)
MaxAuthTries 3 — lock out after 3 failed attempts
MaxSessions 10 — enough for AI agent parallel SSH (increase to 20 for heavy agent use)

antipattern.description: Setting AllowUsers in multiple drop-in files. The last file alphabetically wins, silently excluding users set in earlier files.
antipattern.reason: Consolidate AllowUsers into a single drop-in file to avoid invisible user lockout.

rule.statement: Restrict KexAlgorithms to curve25519 and sntrup761 hybrid. Restrict Ciphers to AEAD-only (chacha20-poly1305, aes256-gcm, aes128-gcm). Remove weak host keys (RSA, ECDSA) and keep only ssh_host_ed25519_key.
rule.rationale: Default OpenSSH includes legacy algorithms for compatibility. Restricting to modern algorithms prevents downgrade attacks. AEAD ciphers provide both encryption and authentication without separate MAC negotiation.

rule.statement: Always run sudo sshd -t to validate config syntax before restarting sshd. Follow with sudo sshd -T to show the effective merged configuration including all drop-in files.
rule.rationale: sshd -t validates syntax but not runtime issues (missing host key file). Always follow with systemctl status ssh after restart to confirm the daemon is running.
